title: Chettinad Chicken Curry

subs.name: 750 g mutton curry cut — pressure-cook 5 whistles before adding to masala

ingredients.name: gingelly oil (nallennai)
visual_checks: any neutral oil works but changes character
ingredients.notes: any neutral oil works but changes character

subs.name: coconut oil or neutral cooking oil
subs.notes: gingelly oil is the Chettinad signature

subs.name: 80 ml thick coconut milk added with the water at step 7

steps.text: Dry roast the fennel seeds and black peppercorns in the kadai on low flame, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until fragrant and lightly darkened. Transfer to a mixie, add grated coconut and 3 tablespoons of water, and grind to a smooth paste. Set aside.

Wipe the kadai, pour in the gingelly oil and heat on medium. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter, then drop in the star anise, cinnamon and cloves. Stir for 30 seconds until the whole spices release their fragrance.
Add the curry leaves and sliced shallots. Fry on medium heat, stirring often, until the shallots turn deep golden and caramelised at the edges, about 7 minutes.

Add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 2 minutes until the raw smell disappears. Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook, pressing occasionally, until they completely break down and oil begins to separate, about 5 minutes.
Reduce to low flame. Add red chilli powder, coriander powder and turmeric. Stir continuously for 30 seconds to bloom the spices — do not let the powders scorch.

Add the chicken pieces and raise the flame to high. Toss to coat all pieces in masala and sear for 5 minutes, turning regularly, until the chicken loses its pink colour and is coated in the dark masala.
Pour in 400 ml hot water and season with salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to medium, cover and simmer for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the oil begins to rise.
Stir in the coconut-pepper paste and mix well. Simmer uncovered for 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the gravy deepens in colour and a thin slick of oil floats on the surface.

Taste and correct the salt. Switch off the flame, scatter coriander leaves over the top and rest for 10 minutes before serving — the flavour improves as it sits. Serve with idiyappam, parotta or steamed rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: shallot → 1 large onion, finely sliced (shallots give deeper sweetness); gingelly-oil → coconut oil or neutral cooking oil (gingelly oil is the Chettinad signature); coconut → 80 ml thick coconut milk added with the water at step 7; chicken → 750 g mutton curry cut — pressure-cook 5 whistles before adding to masala
